Pre-Prelaunch– QUICKstart GUIDE
Pre-Prelaunch: the shot across the bow
Overview
If you want to build a product that your prospect truly wants and then get them to buy it, you
should engage them in a conversation or dialogue. This conversation starts in the Pre-
Prelaunch Sequence of the Product Launch Formula. The whole idea of the Pre-Prelaunch is
to begin to activate your tribe – or start building a tribe if you don’t have one yet.
Your Pre-Prelaunch Sequence is all about grabbing your market’s attention without actually
trying to sell them anything at all. In the Pre-Prelaunch, you start conversations that will
help you:
§ 	Warm your list.
§ 	Figure out some of the primary objections people will have.
§ 	Craft and refine your product and offer.
§ 	Gather testimonials.
§ 	Judge how receptive the market will be to your offer.
§ 	Create anticipation and buzz.
§ 	Attract JV partners.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 6 --

ProductLaunchFormula.com 	© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc.
Pre-Prelaunch– QUICKstart GUIDE
Identifying what your prospect wants
A great way to start the conversation that can help you to craft and/or refine your offer is
by simply asking your audience what they’re interested in. You do this in a generic email
query. The elements of this strategy include:
1. 	At the bottom of one of your normal emails (blog post updated, free training,
monthly newsletter, etc.), add a simple request.
2. 	Say “Finally, I have a question for you. If you could have a private conversation
with me, what two questions would you like to ask me?”
3. 	Provide an email address for you that looks private like it would go directly to you.
4. 	As a P.S. add “I’m really serious – go ahead and push the reply button now and
tell me your question. What do you need help with?”
5. 	Use the responses to help you understand your prospects wants and needs and
begin to craft or refine a solution that addresses the common questions asked.
Testing an Idea
Once you have an idea for your offer (or your Prelaunch content), you can test it with
another simple email. Similar to the last strategy, you can test your idea with the following
steps:
1. 	At the bottom of one of your normal emails (blog post updated, free training,
monthly newsletter, etc.), ask for feedback.
2. 	Say “I’ve never revealed {your topic} before, but I think that maybe I should
based on some of the feedback I’ve been getting recently. If you would like to see
me put together {a free report, a video, a program, etc. on your topic}, then
write back to me. Just hit your reply button and let me know.”
3. 	Use the responses to help you validate if there is demand for your offer/Prelaunch
content or if you need to refine your idea.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 6 --

ProductLaunchFormula.com 	© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc.
Pre-Prelaunch– QUICKstart GUIDE
The SHOT ACROSS THE BOW
The Pre-Prelaunch is called the “Shot across the bow,” which is a naval term for a
warning shot fired toward another ship. The whole idea is to get the other ship’s attention
without resorting to an overt attack.
The most effective way to let your prospects know something is coming without selling
them anything, is through a simple formula that includes a survey. Here are the essential
elements for this Pre-Prelaunch Strategy:
1. 	Send an email: Email your list with the subject line: “Quick announcement and
a favor...” (Warms your list, creates anticipation.)
2. 	Ask for their help: Keep the email simple but let readers know you’re close to
wrapping up a long awaited program/training, but before you do, you have a
couple questions to ask, and can they help you out?
3. 	Send them to a survey: Include a live link to a survey (Survey Monkey, Google
Forms, etc.).
4. 	Tell them about your program: On the survey page, provide some basics
about what the program is, what’s included, what the benefit will be, and tell your
audience that before you finalize the program, you need to make sure you have
covered everything and you need their help.
5. 	Ask for objections: Ask them to take a few minutes to provide their top
questions about {the topic you’re presenting}. (Provides info to craft and refine
your product and offer.)
6. 	Ask them if they’re interested: On the survey thank you page, thank them for
their response and tell them if they want to be contacted when the program is
available, to leave their name and email address. (Helps to judge how receptive the
market is.)
7. 	Gather testimonials with the “Testimonial Cruncher”: On the third
screen of the survey, tell them the survey is over and say that while you have lots of
raving fans, because this upcoming program is new, you don’t yet have
testimonials. Ask if they’d be willing to leave a testimonial for the
products/services they’ve received from you.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 6 --

ProductLaunchFormula.com 	© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc.
Pre-Prelaunch– QUICKstart GUIDE
8. 	Provide examples: Provide a few examples/model testimonials to make it easy
for them to know what you’re looking for. Again, ask if they’d be willing to provide
a testimonial and if they do it may even appear on your webpage giving them
attribution. (Allows you to gather testimonials before you launch your product.)
Building Buzz
In order to create buzz and get the market talking about you and generating interest for
your product before you even launch, you have to strategically create buzz. Here are some
ways to create buzz:
§ 	Email your JV partners: Before you start the Prelaunch, let them know
something is coming, just a quick heads up. Give them a few details about when
the Prelaunch and the launch start. Let them know about 6 weeks ahead of time
and then remind them each week with more information until the Prelaunch
starts.
§ 	Email your existing clients: Several weeks before your Prelaunch, ask for
case studies.
§ 	Make yourself available: Do interviews and consider speaking gigs during the
weeks leading up to your Prelaunch.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

Pre-Prelaunch– QUICKstart GUIDE
Pre-Prelaunch CHECKLIST
Note: You don’t have to do ALL these things every time. But it’s a great checklist to work from.
¨ Did you ask your readers to send you questions about what they’d like to learn?
¨ Did you test your idea by asking your audience for feedback?
¨ Did you base the development of your product and offer on knowing your avatar
and what will solve their biggest challenges?
¨ Did you send the shot across the bow email?
¨ Did you use the survey responses to refine your product and offer?
¨ Did you use language in your offer that your prospect used in their survey?
¨ Did you ask people for their email if they wanted to be notified of when your
product would be available?
¨ Did you get enough interest on the survey to launch your offer?
¨ Did you ask for testimonials at the end of your survey?
¨ When asking for testimonials, did you indicate that their name and city would be
used on your website?
¨ Did you notify your potential JV partners of the upcoming launch well ahead of
time with multiple emails?
¨ Did you ask existing clients for testimonials several weeks before your launch?
¨ Did you make yourself available for interviews and speaking gigs in the weeks
leading up to the launch?
